bibliographic_data.title: Partisan US News Media Representations of Syrian Refugees
bibliographic_data.abstract: We investigate how representations of Syrian refugees (2011-2021) differ across US partisan news outlets. We analyze 47,388 articles from the online US media about Syrian refugees to detail differences in reporting between left- and right-leaning media. Our polarization and question answering results indicated that left-leaning media tended to represent refugees as child victims, welcome in the US, while right-leaning media cast refugees as Islamic terrorists. We noted similar results with our sentiment and offensive speech scores over time, which detail possibly unfavorable representations of refugees in right-leaning media. A strength of our work is how the different techniques we have applied validate each other. Based on our results, we provide several recommendations.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study explores differences in reporting between left- and right-leaning media outlets regarding Syrian refugees from 2011 to 2021, using various NLP techniques.
core_content.key_methodology: 47,388 articles were analyzed using NLP techniques including polarization analysis and question answering. The results showed that left-leaning media tended to represent refugees as child victims and welcome in the US, while right-leaning media cast them as Islamic terrorists.
outcomes: Left-leaning media represented refugees as child victims and welcome in the US.
Right-leaning media portrayed refugees as Islamic terrorists.
Sentiment analysis indicated unfavorable representations of refugees in right-leaning media over time.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The study investigates how Syrian refugee representations differ across partisan news outlets in the United States from 2011 to 2021. By analyzing 47,388 articles, the research highlights that left-leaning media tended to portray refugees as child victims and welcome in the US, while right-leaning media depicted them as Islamic terrorists. Sentiment analysis further confirmed unfavorable representations of refugees in right-leaning media over time. The study provides recommendations for stakeholders to intervene around refugee representations and improve public perception.
